text: Jacob went on his way, and angels of God met him.
Seeing them, Jacob said this is God's camp, and named the place Mahanaim.
Jacob sent messengers ahead to his brother Esau in the land of Seir, the country of Edom.
He instructed them to tell Esau he had been staying with Laban and had returned just now.
He had oxen, donkeys, flocks, servants, and hoped to find favor in Esau's sight.
The messengers returned saying they had gone to Esau, who was coming to meet him with four hundred men.
Jacob was greatly afraid and distressed, so he divided his people, flocks, and camels into two camps.
He reasoned that if Esau attacked one camp, the other could escape.
Jacob prayed, calling on the God of Abraham and Isaac, the LORD who told him to return to his country.
He said he was unworthy of all the kindness God had shown him, since he crossed the Jordan with only a staff and now had two camps.
He begged to be delivered from Esau, fearing he would come and attack them all, including the mothers and children.
He reminded God of the promise to make his offspring like the sand of the sea, too numerous to count.
Jacob spent the night there, then selected a gift for Esau from what he had.
He chose goats, sheep, camels with their young, and cattle in generous numbers.
He set apart a large gift of livestock in total.
He put them in the care of his servants, herd by herd, and told them to go ahead of him with space between each herd.
He instructed the first servant that when Esau met him and asked whose he was, to answer accordingly.
He should say they were Jacob's gift sent to his lord Esau, and that Jacob was following behind.
He gave the same instructions to the second and third servants and all who followed the herds.
He hoped to pacify Esau with the gifts sent ahead, then to see his face and perhaps be accepted.
So the gift went on ahead of him, while he himself spent that night in the camp.
During the night he got up and sent his two wives, two maids, and eleven children across the ford of the Jabbok.
He sent them across the stream along with all he had.
Jacob was left alone, and a man wrestled with him until daybreak.
When the man saw he could not overpower Jacob, he touched his hip and put it out of joint as they wrestled.
The man asked to be let go since dawn was breaking, but Jacob refused to release him without a blessing.
The man asked his name, and he said Jacob.
The man said his name would no longer be Jacob but Israel, since he had struggled with God and with men and prevailed.
Jacob asked the man's name; he replied why do you ask, and blessed him there.
Jacob named the place Peniel, saying he had seen God face to face and survived.
The sun rose as he passed Peniel, limping because of his hip.
To this day the Israelites do not eat the tendon near the hip socket, because of what happened to Jacob.
